documents.title: Michigan State University public AI hub or guidance
Michigan State University AI policy/guidance source

ai_strategy.summary: Michigan State University's public AI posture is visible through governance guidance, teaching resources, research activity, and enterprise/tool support. The evidence points to a pragmatic R1 approach: encourage AI literacy and research while constraining sensitive data and academic-integrity risks.

key_provisions: Use AI in ways consistent with academic integrity and existing university policy.
Protect sensitive, confidential, student, research, or regulated data when using AI tools.
Faculty or academic units retain responsibility for clear course-level expectations where applicable.
Human review and accountability remain necessary for AI-assisted work.
ai_policy.summary: Michigan State University has public AI guidance or policy-adjacent materials. The cited guidance addresses responsible use, data protection, ethics, and course or work-context expectations.

bodies.name: MSU AI guidelines and Ethics Institute values
bodies.scope: Coordinates or evidences institutional AI governance, guidance, research, teaching, or tool enablement.

governance_structure.summary: Public evidence identifies MSU AI guidelines and Ethics Institute values as the main AI governance or coordination signal for Michigan State University.

initiatives.title: MSU AI guidelines and Ethics Institute values AI governance and guidance
initiatives.description: Michigan State University publishes AI guidance or governance resources that frame responsible use, academic integrity, privacy, and institutional oversight. The cited materials make AI a university-level issue rather than only a classroom technology topic.
initiatives.significance: This is a material public signal of how an R1 university is translating AI from discussion into institutional practice.

collaborations.description: No external collaboration was identified in the cited source.

initiatives.policy_implications: The public guidance creates an institutional baseline for course, research, and administrative AI use.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Michigan State University responsible AI and access signal
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Cited public materials connect AI adoption to privacy, bias, academic integrity, access, community benefit, or responsible human use.

regulatory_context.summary: Michigan public AI activity is relevant context; this profile treats campus policy and institutional evidence as the primary governance layer.

narrative_summary: Michigan State University presents an AI posture typical of an active R1 institution: public guidance for generative AI, visible teaching resources, research activity, and a growing set of supported tools or infrastructure. The most mature signals are governance and responsible-use guidance, teaching and syllabus support, and research capacity through AI4ED at CREATE for STEM Institute. Generative AI guidance for academic programs gives the institution a workforce-development or curriculum signal, while Microsoft Copilot with enterprise data protection shows that AI is also being handled as an enterprise technology and data-governance issue. Public sources also show attention to responsible use, access, bias, privacy, or community impact, though some operational details remain incomplete. In particular, current public pages do not consistently disclose named executive ownership, all procurement decisions, full user counts, or complete dollar amounts. This profile therefore treats the institution as operational and growing rather than fully transformative: AI is embedded across policy, pedagogy, research, and tools, but not every part of the campus AI operating model is publicly documented.

gaps_acknowledged: Leadership names, enrollment, and exact enterprise AI user counts were not filled unless directly necessary; public pages were stronger for initiatives than current-role rosters.
Some initiatives cite policy or hub pages because a single dated strategy document was not publicly identified.
No comprehensive AI budget was identified beyond explicitly cited grants or public funding signals.
Follow-up search closed platform_usage_or_contracts at product/scope level for Copilot, Gemini and NotebookLM, but no MSU public user counts or contract values were found.
Follow-up search found HPCC as a named compute resource and AI-Ready Spartans grants, but no public GPU count or total AI investment amount.
